title: SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_9 -- "SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling claim_*_proof_*.tex file).
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_9 (statement)
% title: SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_9}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_9}

\begin{Rem}[SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder]
    For a CADMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$, also $G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic.
    If $<$ is any topological order of $G$ given by enumerating all nodes $v \in J \cup V$ via:
    \[ v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n,\]
    then, for instance, 
    a topological order for $G_{\swig(W)}$ can be achieved by assigning for a node $v_j \in W$ with index $j$ the node 
    $v_j^o$ the index $j-\frac{1}{3}$
    and $v_j^i$ the index $j+\frac{1}{3}$, and then ordering all nodes according to their index value.
\end{Rem}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html: For a CADMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$, also $G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic. If $<$ is any topological order of $G$ given by enumerating all nodes $v \in J \cup V$ via: \[ v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n,\] then, for instance, a topological order for $G_{\swig(W)}$ can be achieved by assigning for a node $v_j \in W$ with index $j$ the node $v_j^o$ the index $j-\frac{1}{3}$ and $v_j^i$ the index $j+\frac{1}{3}$, and then ordering all nodes according to their index value.

% Claim proof file for row claim_3_9 -- "SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_9 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_9}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_9}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder]
    For a CADMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$, also $G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic.
    If $<$ is any topological order of $G$ given by enumerating all nodes $v \in J \cup V$ via:
    \[ v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n,\]
    then, for instance, 
    a topological order for $G_{\swig(W)}$ can be achieved by assigning for a node $v_j \in W$ with index $j$ the node 
    $v_j^o$ the index $j-\frac{1}{3}$
    and $v_j^i$ the index $j+\frac{1}{3}$, and then ordering all nodes according to their index value.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
The whole argument rests on the observation, already spelt out in the closing paragraph of \refrow{def_3_12} (\emph{``we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$''}), that the SWIG is a node-splitting followed by a hard intervention on the resulting inner copies. Concretely, identifying the SWIG superscripts with the node-splitting superscripts via $v^o = v^0$ and $v^i = v^1$ (both \refrow{def_3_11} and \refrow{def_3_12} construct $W^0, W^1$ resp.\ $W^o, W^i$ as ``two disjoint copies of $W$'', and both use the convention $v^o = v^i = v$ resp.\ $v^0 = v^1 = v$ for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$), we have
\[
  G_{\swig(W)} \;=\; \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}.
\]
Write $G' := G_{\spl(W)}$. We verify the four bullets of \refrow{def_3_12} by composing \refrow{def_3_11} with \refrow{def_3_10}.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Input nodes.} By \refrow{def_3_11}.i, $J' = J$. By \refrow{def_3_10}.i, $J'_{\doit(W^i)} = J' \cup W^i = J \cup W^i = J \dcup W^i = J_{\swig(W)}$ (the last two equalities use the disjointness of the fresh copy $W^i$ from $J$ asserted in \refrow{def_3_12}, and item i. of \refrow{def_3_12}).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Output nodes.} By \refrow{def_3_11}.ii, $V' = (V \sm W) \dcup W^o \dcup W^i$. By \refrow{def_3_10}.ii, $V'_{\doit(W^i)} = V' \sm W^i = (V \sm W) \dcup W^o = V_{\swig(W)}$ (item ii. of \refrow{def_3_12}).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Directed edges.} By \refrow{def_3_11}.iii,
\[
  E' \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC w^o \tuh w^i \st w \in W \rC.
\]
By \refrow{def_3_10}.iii, $E'_{\doit(W^i)}$ removes every edge whose head lies in $W^i$. For an edge $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o$ the head $v_2^o$ lies in $W^o \dcup (V \sm W)$, never in $W^i$; for an edge $w^o \tuh w^i$ the head $w^i$ lies in $W^i$ and the edge is removed. The surviving edges are
\[
  E'_{\doit(W^i)} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;=\; E_{\swig(W)}
\]
(item iii. of \refrow{def_3_12}).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Bidirected edges.} By \refrow{def_3_11}.iv, $L' = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$. By \refrow{def_3_10}.iv, $L'_{\doit(W^i)}$ removes every bidirected edge with an endpoint in $W^i$. Since $v_1^o, v_2^o \in W^o \dcup (V \sm W)$, neither endpoint lies in $W^i$ and nothing is removed: $L'_{\doit(W^i)} = L' = L_{\swig(W)}$ (item iv. of \refrow{def_3_12}).

\medskip
The four bullets agree, so $G_{\swig(W)} = \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$. The two halves of the claim are now corollaries of \refrow{claim_3_6} (the node-splitting analogue) and \refrow{claim_3_3} (the hard-intervention analogue).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part A (topological order).}
Let $<$ be a topological order of $G$ (\refrow{def_3_8}) enumerating $J \cup V$ as $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n$. By \refrow{claim_3_6} (Part A: node-splitting preserves the topological order via the $\pm 1/3$ interleave), the relation $<_\spl$ obtained by assigning, for each $v_j \in W$ with index $j$, the indices
\[
  v_j^0 \mapsto j - \tfrac{1}{3}, \qquad v_j^1 \mapsto j + \tfrac{1}{3},
\]
keeping the index $j$ for every $v_j \in (J \cup V) \sm W$, and ordering nodes by index value, is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$. Under the identification $v^o = v^0$, $v^i = v^1$ (established at the start of the proof), this is precisely the $\pm 1/3$ recipe stated in the claim: $v_j^o$ at index $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ and $v_j^i$ at index $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$.

We now lift $<_\spl$ from $G_{\spl(W)}$ to $G_{\swig(W)} = \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$ using \refrow{claim_3_3} (Part B: a hard intervention preserves a topological order, under the precondition that the intervention target lies in the ambient input--output node set). Here the precondition reads $W^i \ins J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, which is met: by \refrow{def_3_11}.ii,
\[
  W^i \;\ins\; (V \sm W) \dcup W^o \dcup W^i \;=\; V_{\spl(W)} \;\ins\; J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}.
\]
Applying \refrow{claim_3_3} (Part B) to $G_{\spl(W)}$ with intervention target $W^i$ yields that $<_\spl$ is also a topological order of $\lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)} = G_{\swig(W)}$. This is the topological order described by the claim.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Part B (acyclicity).}
Assume $G$ is acyclic (\refrow{def_3_6}). By \refrow{claim_3_6} (Part B: node-splitting preserves acyclicity), $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic. By \refrow{claim_3_3} (Part A: hard intervention preserves acyclicity --- no precondition required), $\lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$ is acyclic. By the composite identity $G_{\swig(W)} = \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$ established above, $G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder

For a CADMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$, also $G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic. If $<$ is any topological order of $G$ given by enumerating all nodes $v \in J \cup V$ via: \[ v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n,\] then, for instance, a topological order for $G_{\swig(W)}$ can be achieved by assigning for a node $v_j \in W$ with index $j$ the node $v_j^o$ the index $j-\frac{1}{3}$ and $v_j^i$ the index $j+\frac{1}{3}$, and then ordering all nodes according to their index value.

Proof.

The whole argument rests on the observation, already spelt out in the closing paragraph of def_3_12 (“we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$”), that the SWIG is a node-splitting followed by a hard intervention on the resulting inner copies. Concretely, identifying the SWIG superscripts with the node-splitting superscripts via $v^o = v^0$ and $v^i = v^1$ (both def_3_11 and def_3_12 construct $W^0, W^1$ resp.\ $W^o, W^i$ as “two disjoint copies of $W$”, and both use the convention $v^o = v^i = v$ resp.\ $v^0 = v^1 = v$ for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$), we have \[ G_{\swig(W)} \;=\; \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}. \] Write $G' := G_{\spl(W)}$. We verify the four bullets of def_3_12 by composing def_3_11 with def_3_10.

Input nodes. By def_3_11.i, $J' = J$. By def_3_10.i, $J'_{\doit(W^i)} = J' \cup W^i = J \cup W^i = J \dcup W^i = J_{\swig(W)}$ (the last two equalities use the disjointness of the fresh copy $W^i$ from $J$ asserted in def_3_12, and item i. of def_3_12).

Output nodes. By def_3_11.ii, $V' = (V \sm W) \dcup W^o \dcup W^i$. By def_3_10.ii, $V'_{\doit(W^i)} = V' \sm W^i = (V \sm W) \dcup W^o = V_{\swig(W)}$ (item ii. of def_3_12).

Directed edges. By def_3_11.iii, \[ E' \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC w^o \tuh w^i \st w \in W \rC. \] By def_3_10.iii, $E'_{\doit(W^i)}$ removes every edge whose head lies in $W^i$. For an edge $v_1^i \tuh v_2^o$ the head $v_2^o$ lies in $W^o \dcup (V \sm W)$, never in $W^i$; for an edge $w^o \tuh w^i$ the head $w^i$ lies in $W^i$ and the edge is removed. The surviving edges are \[ E'_{\doit(W^i)} \;=\; \lC v_1^i \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \;=\; E_{\swig(W)} \] (item iii. of def_3_12).

Bidirected edges. By def_3_11.iv, $L' = \lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$. By def_3_10.iv, $L'_{\doit(W^i)}$ removes every bidirected edge with an endpoint in $W^i$. Since $v_1^o, v_2^o \in W^o \dcup (V \sm W)$, neither endpoint lies in $W^i$ and nothing is removed: $L'_{\doit(W^i)} = L' = L_{\swig(W)}$ (item iv. of def_3_12).

The four bullets agree, so $G_{\swig(W)} = \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$. The two halves of the claim are now corollaries of claim_3_6 (the node-splitting analogue) and claim_3_3 (the hard-intervention analogue).

Part A (topological order). Let $<$ be a topological order of $G$ (def_3_8) enumerating $J \cup V$ as $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n$. By claim_3_6 (Part A: node-splitting preserves the topological order via the $\pm 1/3$ interleave), the relation $<_\spl$ obtained by assigning, for each $v_j \in W$ with index $j$, the indices \[ v_j^0 \mapsto j - \tfrac{1}{3}, \qquad v_j^1 \mapsto j + \tfrac{1}{3}, \] keeping the index $j$ for every $v_j \in (J \cup V) \sm W$, and ordering nodes by index value, is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$. Under the identification $v^o = v^0$, $v^i = v^1$ (established at the start of the proof), this is precisely the $\pm 1/3$ recipe stated in the claim: $v_j^o$ at index $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ and $v_j^i$ at index $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$.

We now lift $<_\spl$ from $G_{\spl(W)}$ to $G_{\swig(W)} = \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$ using claim_3_3 (Part B: a hard intervention preserves a topological order, under the precondition that the intervention target lies in the ambient input–output node set). Here the precondition reads $W^i \ins J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, which is met: by def_3_11.ii, \[ W^i \;\ins\; (V \sm W) \dcup W^o \dcup W^i \;=\; V_{\spl(W)} \;\ins\; J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}. \] Applying claim_3_3 (Part B) to $G_{\spl(W)}$ with intervention target $W^i$ yields that $<_\spl$ is also a topological order of $\lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)} = G_{\swig(W)}$. This is the topological order described by the claim.

Part B (acyclicity). Assume $G$ is acyclic (def_3_6). By claim_3_6 (Part B: node-splitting preserves acyclicity), $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic. By claim_3_3 (Part A: hard intervention preserves acyclicity — no precondition required), $\lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$ is acyclic. By the composite identity $G_{\swig(W)} = \lp G_{\spl(W)} \rp_{\doit(W^i)}$ established above, $G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- claim_3_9 part A: if `W ⊆ G.V` and `r` is a topological order of
`G`, then `splitOrder W r` is a topological order of
`G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW` (the SWIG). Mirrors the
constructive half of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_12 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (line 612), with the LN's
`± 1/3` interleaving recipe for `v_j^o` / `v_j^i` encoded as the
four-case `splitOrder` pattern match -- the *same* `splitOrder W r`
relation produced by claim_3_6 (whose recipe for `v_j^0` / `v_j^1`
agrees with the SWIG's `v_j^o` / `v_j^i` under our
`^o = ^0 = Sum.inl`, `^i = ^1 = Sum.inr` convention).

The `W ⊆ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` itself (def_3_12), inherited from
`nodeSplittingOn` def_3_11; it is *not* a topological-order
condition.

See `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` below for the
acyclicity half of the LN remark and the file-level docstring for
the rationale behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into two
theorems. This row is the corollary chain of claim_3_3 + claim_3_6;
both halves are short composites of the existing theorems. -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {r : α → α → Prop} (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder
      (splitOrder W r) := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_9_proof_SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder.tex`
  -- Part A. The SWIG is by definition `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW
  -- ).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)`; chain claim_3_6
  -- part A (`isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn`, producing
  -- `splitOrder W r` on the split graph) with claim_3_3 part B
  -- (`isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn`, transporting any
  -- topological order through HI provided the intervention target
  -- lies in the ambient `J ∪ V`). The HI precondition reads
  -- `Set.range Sum.inr ⊆ (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).J ∪
  -- (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).V`, which holds because the fresh
  -- `Sum.inr` copies sit in the split graph's *output* set by
  -- `nodeSplittingOn_V`.
  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).IsTopologicalOrder (splitOrder W r)
  refine isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn ?_
    (isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn hW hr)
  rintro x ⟨w, rfl⟩
  rw [nodeSplittingOn_J, nodeSplittingOn_V]
  exact Or.inr (Or.inr ⟨w, rfl⟩)

/-- claim_3_9 part B: if `W ⊆ G.V` and `G` is acyclic, then
`G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW` (the SWIG) is acyclic.
Mirrors the acyclicity half of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_12
in `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (line 612); see
`isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` above for the
constructive half and the file-level docstring for the rationale
behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into two theorems.

The `W ⊆ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` itself (def_3_12); see
`isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`. No `[Finite α]`
hypothesis is added at the statement phase, keeping both proof routes
open (composite via claim_3_6 part B + claim_3_3 part A, or direct
walk-lifting on the SWIG). -/
theorem isAcyclic_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).IsAcyclic := by
  -- Mirrors `tex/claim_3_9_proof_SwigAcyclicTopologicalOrder.tex`
  -- Part B. The SWIG is by definition `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW
  -- ).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)`; chain claim_3_6
  -- part B (`isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn`, NS preserves acyclicity)
  -- with claim_3_3 part A (`isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn`, HI
  -- preserves acyclicity, no precondition).
  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).IsAcyclic
  exact isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn _ (isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn hW h)
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {r : α → α → Prop} (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder
      (splitOrder W r) := by

  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).IsTopologicalOrder (splitOrder W r)
  refine isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn ?_
    (isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn hW hr)
  rintro x ⟨w, rfl⟩
  rw [nodeSplittingOn_J, nodeSplittingOn_V]
  exact Or.inr (Or.inr ⟨w, rfl⟩)

theorem isAcyclic_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).IsAcyclic := by

  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).IsAcyclic
  exact isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn _ (isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn hW h)
lean_explanation:
The Lean encoding lives in the `Causality.CDMG` namespace and quantifies over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*` — the LN's silent "$G$ is a CADMG over some carrier". The base graph `G : CDMG α` matches the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$, and `W : Set α` is the intervention set on which the SWIG construction is performed. The SWIG itself, $G_{\swig(W)}$, is `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`, which by def 3.12 unfolds to the composition `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)` — first split every $w \in W$ into a pair $w^o = $ `Sum.inl w` and $w^i = $ `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩`, then hard-intervene on the fresh `Sum.inr`-copies.

The LN's remark is split into two named theorems, both quantifying over the same ambient pieces. Part A, `isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`, takes the precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.V` and a topological order `hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r` of $G$ (the four-field bundle `irrefl` / `trans` / `trichotomous` / `parent_lt` from def 3.8), and concludes that `splitOrder W r` is a topological order of the SWIG. Part B, `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`, takes `hW` together with `h : G.IsAcyclic` and concludes that the SWIG is acyclic. In both halves `G`, `W`, `r` are implicit (recovered by unification from `hW` / `hr` / the conclusion); `hW`, `hr`, `h` are explicit.

A few Mathlib idioms are worth flagging. The SWIG's carrier is the disjoint sum `α ⊕ ↑W` — the same carrier as the post-split graph, since hard intervention is carrier-preserving — where `↑W` is the coercion of the set `W` to its subtype `{ x // x ∈ W }`. `Sum.inl : α → α ⊕ ↑W` and `Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W` are the two summand injections, encoding the LN's $W^o = W^0$ and $W^i = W^1$ copies respectively. `Set.range Sum.inr` is the set of all fresh $W^i$ vertices in the split carrier — exactly the LN's intervention target inside the def-3-12 unfolding. `splitOrder W r` is the noncomputable relation introduced in claim 3.6: a four-case pattern match on `α ⊕ ↑W` that lifts `r` to the split carrier and implements the LN's $\pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ interleaving recipe purely relationally, without committing to any real-valued index function.

In shape: each theorem takes two hypotheses and concludes one predicate on the SWIG. The proofs are short — both rewrite the SWIG into its NS-then-HI unfolding and chain the corresponding halves of claim 3.6 and claim 3.3.
design_choices:
**The LN's single `\Rem` is split into two named theorems.** Forré–Mooij bundle "$G_{\swig(W)}$ is acyclic" and "an explicit topological order on $G_{\swig(W)}$" under one paragraph. The Lean encoding splits them because the two halves serve different downstream consumers. The topological-order half is the *named-construction* result — counterfactual and iSCM chapters want `splitOrder W r` by name to plug into their own machinery for evaluating mechanisms along the inherited order. The acyclicity half is the *side-condition* result — do-calculus, ID-algorithm, and causal-discovery layers want acyclicity of the SWIG as a hypothesis without committing to a specific topological order. A bundled conjunction would force each class either to project or to drag in data the other class does not need. The split mirrors the precedents at the node-splitting and hard-intervention rows.

**`splitOrder W r` is reused by name, not redefined on the SWIG.** The SWIG's carrier is the same `α ⊕ ↑W` as the intermediate post-split graph, because hard intervention is carrier-preserving. The `splitOrder` relation is purely typed on the carrier and parameterised by `W : Set α` and `r : α → α → Prop`, so it has the right type to live on the SWIG directly. The LN's $\pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ recipe for $v_j^o$ / $v_j^i$ is identical to its recipe for $v_j^0$ / $v_j^1$ (modulo notation) under the convention $v^o = v^0 = $ `Sum.inl v`, $v^i = v^1 = $ `Sum.inr ⟨v, hv⟩`. Redefining the relation here would produce a syntactic alias of the existing one; reusing it by name lets downstream callers talk about a *single* named relation regardless of whether they reach the SWIG via the composition or directly.

**The `W ⊆ G.V` precondition is structural, not transport-specific.** Both halves carry the same `hW`. The precondition is required simply because `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` itself demands it (inherited from the node-splitting step, whose fresh edge $w^o \to w^i$ needs $w \in V$ so that the source `Sum.inl w` lives in the split graph's output set). Unlike claim 3.3's transport for plain hard intervention — where the topological-order half requires $W \subseteq J \cup V$ but the acyclicity half does not — here the precondition is structural and uniform across both halves.

**`G.IsAcyclic` stays as a separate `Prop`-valued hypothesis, not bundled into the carrier or into Part A.** `CDMG α` is the LN's *unconditional* DMG carrier; the *A* in CADMG is modelled as a `Prop`-valued predicate `IsAcyclic` rather than a structure field or typeclass. Bundling acyclicity into the carrier would force every chapter-3 lemma either to restate the typeclass instance or to commit early to a refinement that not-yet-acyclic precursors in later chapters would need to relax. Part A's topological-order hypothesis already implies the SWIG's acyclicity, so adding `h : G.IsAcyclic` to Part A would be redundant; Part B is the dual, for callers holding acyclicity without a named order on hand.

**No `[Finite α]` instance hypothesis, and names follow Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>` convention.** Both composite proof routes are finiteness-free, which lets later chapters apply the results over not-yet-finitised vertex types. The names `isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` and `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` mirror their precedents from node-splitting and hard intervention. The long-form name is preferred over the `swig` alias so the lemma name matches the underlying `def`; callers can still invoke the lemmas through the `swig` alias by reducibility.
